subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Constitutional Checks and Balances vs. Social Movements in Representing the People's Will
stimulus: An important feature of a democratic republic is that it represents the will of the people. Take a position on whether constitutional checks and balances or citizen participation in social movements is more effective in ensuring the people's will is represented.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Article I of the United States Constitution, The Federalist 10, "Letter from a Birmingham Jail".)
parts.scoring_notes: Thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. Does NOT need foundational documents.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Checks and balances are more effective in ensuring the people’s will is represented because they guarantee that parts of the government that defy the people will be counteracted or removed entirely.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Social movements are more effective because they act independently of the government and can force the government to respond to the will of the people.”

wrong_examples: “History has shown that citizen participation in social movements ensures that the people’s will is represented.”
“Checks and balances are a feature of the Constitution.”
common_traps: Do NOT require foundational documents in thesis
A because clause with reasons IS a line of reasoning

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: Award HIGHEST tier met. Each tier 1pt progressive. Tier 2+ requires Row A.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: One relevant piece of evidence.
Evidence Tier 2: One specific piece supporting thesis OR two relevant. Requires Row A.

required_elements: One specific piece supporting thesis OR two relevant
alternatives.correct_example: Uses evidence to directly support thesis.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Two specific pieces supporting thesis, one from listed foundational document. Requires Row A.

alternatives.correct_example: Uses foundational doc + concept supporting thesis.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain the logical connection between evidence and the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “‘In Letter from a Birmingham Jail,’ King explains that waiting for the government to check itself has led to greater suffering for African Americans therefore, social movements increase pressure on policymakers to gain outcomes favorable to their cause.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Article I provides Congress with the power to override a presidential veto. This power can be very effective in representing the will of the people, because it can be used to stop a president that vetoes legislation for selfish or personal reasons.”

common_traps: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B
Simply restating evidence without reasoning does not earn this point

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “While social movements are broadly based and can pressure the government to respond to what the people want, this is not the same as holding elected officials accountable to the people. Thus, social movements are more limited in what they can achieve, while checks and balances are able to protect against a much larger set of problems.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “While checks and balances are designed to hold the government accountable to the will of the people, they can also be used to ignore the will of the people. Because social movements are motivated by the people’s desire to hold the government accountable, they are more effective.”

wrong_examples: “Many would argue that social movements are better, but this is simply untrue.”
“Some would argue that social movements are better because they can put extra pressure on government to represent the will of the people.”
common_traps: Simply identifying an alternate perspective without rebutting/refuting it does not earn this point
